channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Ecco the Dolphin: Complete Includes Old-School Ecco and a Brand New Game
item.content:encoded:
Developer A&R Atelier and publisher Sega have announced Ecco the Dolphin: Complete, a "comprehensive new package" that aims both to honor the past of the titular mammal and to bring a brand new game into the fold as well.

We first got wind of Ecco the Dolphin's revival back in May last year (assuming you don't count the Intellivision Amico game supposedly from the original team, of course), and now, we know what that revival will look like.

Per a press release, Ecco the Dolphin: Complete will aim to represent "the complete, definitive Ecco the Dolphin experience", and it'll do so on two fronts: first, by including lots of ways to play the original games, and second, by also including a brand new adventure.

A&R Atelier says that Ecco the Dolphin: Complete will include "all versions" of both the original game and sequel The Tides of Time, "preserved and freely explorable", although it won't include 2000's Ecco the Dolphin: Defender of the Future, presumably due to creator Ed Annunziata's lack of involvement.

In addition to these games, the collection will also include "a brand-new contemporary Ecco game" that A&R says "extends the journey into the modern era". The studio hasn't revealed any additional information about what that new game will look like, however.

Games included as part of Ecco the Dolphin: Complete will pack in "built-in speedrunning support, achievements, and leaderboards", according to A&R, and there will also be "meta quests" that "span across the original games and the new contemporary title".

You'll also be able to build your own custom courses, but before you get too excited, it sounds like that means custom configurations of existing Ecco levels rather than entirely new stages. Boo.

Sadly, no release date has been announced for Ecco the Dolphin: Complete yet, nor do we know which platforms it'll be on when it does land. Stay tuned for more on this one.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/ecco-the-dolphin-complete-retro-new-game
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Expanse: Osiris Reborn Preview - Massively Effective
item.content:encoded:
Having shown the world isometric CRPGs aren't going out of style with two Pathfinder games and the excellent Warhammer 40k: Rogue Trader, developer Owlcat is now turning its hand to a Mass Effect-style action RPG with The Expanse: Osiris Reborn.

It's set in the world of the popular sci-fi series (books or TV show, take your pick), but you won't need to be familiar with the source material to enjoy this one; it's an original story that features a few nods and winks to The Expanse, but without aiming to alienate newcomers.

Mass Effect's prolonged period of absence has left a niche a mile wide to be filled, and if The Expanse: Osiris Reborn's opening hours are anything to go by, Owlcat is more than capable of getting the job done. I got to check out Osiris Reborn's beta, and I came away extremely impressed.

The Expanse: Osiris Reborn Is a Worthy Mass Effect Successor

Owlcat's intention to pick up where Mass Effect left off in 2017 (or 2012, depending on your personal level of Andromeda pain) is writ large from The Expanse: Osiris Reborn's first few minutes.

The beta lets you tackle a single mission that takes place fairly early on in the game. You and your twin sibling J have just (mass) effected a daring escape from the Eros space station, carrying a stolen ship in tow, and you're about to deal with the consequences.

You'll get a sense for some of Osiris Reborn's key systems and mechanics in the beta. The opening section lets you explore the station of Pinkwater-4 at your leisure before you're thrust into high-octane combat against mercenary troops.

If you're familiar with Mass Effect, Osiris Reborn is going to feel comfortingly familiar to you. From the third-person over-the-shoulder movement to the BioWare-style conversation interface and tactical combat pause, Owlcat isn't subtle about whose homework it's cribbing.

I'm happy to report, then, that The Expanse: Osiris Reborn feels great. Exploring is fun thanks to plenty of optional chests to find and environmental puzzles to solve, and there's a life and a charm to the world here as well.

Visually, Osiris Reborn is beautiful, presenting a gorgeously-realized hard sci-fi world to discover. There are a few teething troubles involving texture pop-in and stutter, but it's early days, and I'm confident Owlcat can address these before the full release.

I'm not familiar with The Expanse, having never really interacted with the books or the show, but I got a strong sense of place from Pinkwater-4 and its inhabitants, and I'm looking forward to learning more when the full launch happens.

Combat and Complexity in The Expanse: Osiris Reborn

If you miss the early days of Mass Effect - that is to say, the less streamlined, setpiece-heavy days - then you're going to find a kindred spirit in The Expanse: Osiris Reborn.

There's an absolute ton of equipment to find and sort through here, including plenty of primary and secondary weapon options and lots of extras to vary up your combat strategy.

I opted to play as a hacker in the beta, and even in this limited gameplay slice, I found lots of ways to change up my abilities, as well as plenty of opportunities to use them against hapless mercenaries.

It probably won't surprise you to learn that combat feels heavily influenced by Mass Effect. It's got the same cover system, as well as the same rhythm built around exploiting ally attacks to open up chances for bullet salvos.

Combat feels rewarding and satisfying in the mission that the beta offers. Groundlings will flank your position, leading to moments of panic, while snipers will watch you like a hawk as you move between cover points.

You can also issue orders to your companion (twin sibling J in this instance), instructing her to flush enemies out of cover or destroy explosive objects for big damage. It's an excellent recreation of Mass Effect's movement-focused third-person RPG shooting.

The Expanse: Osiris Reborn Preview | Final Thoughts

What I played of The Expanse: Osiris Reborn left me hungry for more. There's been no new Mass Effect game for nine years (sorry), and while I like Owlcat's more traditional isometric efforts (and loved Rogue Trader), Osiris Reborn is a much more exciting prospect for me.

It's early days, but The Expanse: Osiris Reborn's loving homage to BioWare's iconic space opera series feels accomplished and confident already. If this beta is anything to go by, Owlcat is in the process of creating something very special indeed.

The Expanse: Osiris Reborn was previewed on PC using a preview build provided by the developer. All screenshots were taken during the preview process.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/expanse-osiris-reborn-preview-massively-effective
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: US Gaming Sales Data for March 2026 Shows Beasties Can't Beat Baseball
item.content:encoded:
Sales analysis firm Circana has released its monthly gaming sales data roundup, and March 2026's data shows that although Resident Evil Requiem put in a strong showing for the month, it can't beat MLB The Show 26.

The baseball sim is the latest entry in the long-running MLB The Show franchise, and given that it launched halfway through the month, its performance is pretty impressive. I guess Americans really do love their baseball, huh?

Resident Evil Requiem is in second place for March, which shouldn't be surprising given its astonishing sales, and third place is occupied by another sports title, namely WWE 2K26. Whether you watch wrestling or not anymore, it looks like it's still popular with a heck of a lot of folks.

Perhaps a little worryingly for Sony and Bungie, extraction shooter Marathon is in fourth place, and it had two weeks longer to carve out a niche for itself than MLB The Show 26 did. Still, Bungie is committing to the game long-term, so it'll be around for a good while yet.

Nintendo's Pokemon Pokopia occupies fifth place, which isn't bad for a single-platform release that's also a spinoff, and sixth place is occupied by the ever-popular Call of Duty: Black Ops 7, with Capcom's new JRPG Monster Hunter Stories 3: Twisted Reflection in seventh.

Eighth place goes to NBA 2K26, and eternally successful voxel sandbox Minecraft is in ninth. Lastly for the top ten, EA Sports FC 26 is in tenth place, but given that everyone who wants to pick that one up probably already has, that's not a surprise.

Despite MLB The Show 26's strong, erm, showing, it still hasn't managed to unseat Resident Evil Requiem from its position at the top of the year-to-date sales chart, although it has shot straight into second place.

When it comes to hardware, the Switch 2 reigned supreme both for March and for the year as a whole so far, with the PS5 naturally ranking second. It's well and truly over for the Xbox Series X|S, it seems, so let's hope Project Helix has some tricks up its sleeve.

As ever, I'd strongly recommend reading Circana's Mat Piscatella's full thread on Bluesky, as it's packed with lots of other interesting insights about the industry and its current status.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/circana-march-2026-us-gaming-sales-data
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Gallipoli Release Date Brings More WWI-Era Shooting Next Month
item.content:encoded:
Developer and publisher BlackMill has announced the release date for Gallipoli, the upcoming fourth installment in its World War I shooter series, and it's not too far away at all.

In a new post on Steam, the studio reveals that the Gallipoli release date is May 21st, with the announcement coming just a few days before Anzac Day (an event to which BlackMill says it wants to dedicate the accompanying release date trailer).

Anzac Day marks the day on which Australian and New Zealander soldiers landed on the Gallipoli Peninsula in Turkiye, marking the first major military action undertaken by soldiers from those territories (both of which were British Imperial dominions at the time).

Rather sweetly, BlackMill says a "small part" of its team is currently at Gallipoli in order to mark the Gallipoli Campaign's anniversary and to pay their respects to "the soldiers from all sides who fought and died there".

As for the game itself, if you're a fan of BlackMill's previous WWI-era games (namely Verdun, Tannenberg, and Isonzo), you'll know what to expect; lots of historically-authentic shooting on accurately-rendered battlegrounds key to the Gallipoli Campaign.

Maps revealed for the game so far include the aforementioned Anzac Cove, as well as the V Beach, Ctesiphon, and Kut Al Amara, the latter two of which come from the game's Mesopotamian campaign.

Of course, plenty of gameplay tweaks have also been introduced in order to make Gallipoli an even more historically-accurate shooter, so if you're in the market for a World War I battlefield sim that respects its source material, your time has come.

You'll be able to check out Gallipoli when it lands on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S on May 21st. If the previous games in the series are anything to go by, you can also expect robust post-launch support and events in the months following release.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/gallipoli-release-date
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
